section_header: 4.2.1 Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City
policy_statement: Develop office-building energy monitoring toward energy-efficiency monitoring by integrating building condition and usage data; connect all City-owned/service properties to the monitoring system.
parent_statement: Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City (key actions related to the service buildings of the City).
verbatim_text:
34. The consumption monitoring of office buildings will be developed towards the monitoring of energy- efficiency by adding the condition and usage data of the buildings into the energy consumption data.

- All properties owned by the City or that offer City services will be connected to the new monitoring system...
extraction_rationale: Specific system development and coverage expansion commitment.
mechanism_description: The City will develop and expand office-building energy monitoring by adding condition and usage data and connecting City-owned/service properties, improving visibility into performance to support energy-efficiency actions.
primary_causal_pathway: Information transparency/market signal → efficiency actions (O&M + retrofits) → ↓ building energy use → ↓ fossil energy combustion → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Cities set or facilitate energy/water benchmarking, monitoring, and/or public reporting so owners, tenants, lenders, and regulators can compare performance and identify inefficiencies.  
(2) Mediating physical/process link: Improved data availability and comparability triggers operational improvements (commissioning, controls tuning), capital upgrades (HVAC, envelope, lighting), and behavioral changes; these measures reduce kWh and on-site fuel consumption for the same building service levels.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower energy demand reduces upstream grid emissions and direct combustion emissions, decreasing overall GHG emissions from the buildings sector.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption leading to lower GHG emissions from electricity generation and on-site fuel use.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: transparency and benchmarking → motivates/identifies efficiency improvements → lowers energy use → lowers GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while water data and energy-burden reporting can support equity and resource efficiency, the core mechanism is to drive building energy efficiency, with the main climate impact being emissions reduction (mitigation).  
3) Edge cases applied: if disclosure is used purely for equity planning or billing assistance without driving efficiency actions, climate impact could be indirect; however, the canonical mechanism specified is explicitly “market signal for efficiency,” keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy that expands benchmarking/monitoring/disclosure to create performance comparisons and motivate efficiency improvements shares the same primary mitigation pathway through reduced building energy use.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether outcomes come mainly from operational changes vs retrofits, and whether electricity vs gas dominates, must be determined at the individual policy level with implementation details and local energy mix.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some related policies emphasize water benchmarking or “energy burden” reporting; those can be primarily equity/resource-planning tools unless explicitly tied to efficiency interventions. Municipal-only monitoring may yield smaller market signaling effects but still targets efficiency-driven emissions reductions.
key_indicators: "consumption monitoring of office buildings"; "monitoring of energy-efficiency"; "adding the condition and usage data"; "energy consumption data"; "All properties owned by the City or that offer City services will be connected"; "new monitoring system"
co_benefits: Improved municipal asset management and maintenance planning; potential operating cost savings
instance_edge_case_notes: Although connection of City properties could be read as an internal mandate, the core instrument is still an information/monitoring system rather than a prescriptive efficiency standard or retrofit requirement.
parent_policy_name: Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City (key actions related to the service buildings of the City).
rescue_reasoning: Climate screen verdict is explicit_self, so we keep unless clearly non-climate. The policy text is directly about energy efficiency in buildings: 'monitoring of energy-efficiency' and connecting all City-owned/service properties to an energy monitoring system. Energy-efficiency measures in buildings are standard mitigation actions via reduced energy use. The gate failure ('no Mitigation outcome token') reflects missing explicit emissions wording, not lack of climate relevance.

section_header: 4.2.1 Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City
policy_statement: Pilot electricity and heating demand response in City premises and develop a plan for adoption scale and schedule based on pilots.
parent_statement: Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City (key actions related to the service buildings of the City).
verbatim_text: 35. Demand response of electricity and heating will be piloted in the City's premises in different types of service building. Based on the pilots, the potential of demand response ... will be estimated, and a plan will be made for the extent and schedule of the adoption of these changes.
extraction_rationale: Concrete pilot-to-scale pathway with planning output.
mechanism_description: The City will pilot electricity and heating demand response in its premises and use the results to plan broader adoption, shifting loads to improve grid flexibility and reduce associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Load shifting/peak reduction → ↓ fossil peaker/marginal generation + ↑ renewable utilization → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: Electricity and heating demand profiles in buildings/facilities are actively managed (e.g., shifting HVAC, thermal storage, water heating, industrial/process loads) in response to price/grid signals or control algorithms.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Lower peak demand and more flexible load reduces dispatch of high-emissions marginal units, avoids inefficient part-load operation, and increases the system’s ability to absorb variable renewable generation (and/or optimize CHP/district heating operations).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels in power/heat generation leading to lower CO₂ (and sometimes reduced upstream methane and local air pollutants), i.e., net GHG emissions reduction.
dominant_pathway_test: Shifting and reducing peak loads lowers reliance on carbon-intensive marginal generation (and improves renewable integration), thereby reducing GHG emissions from electricity/heat supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Demand response primarily affects emissions by changing load shapes so the grid uses less fossil marginal/peaker generation and can integrate more renewables.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: While “flexibility” can improve reliability (an adaptation-like co-benefit) and can improve energy efficiency in some cases, the single biggest climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is typically reduced GHG emissions from electricity/heat supply.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the grid is already near-zero carbon, or if shifted load moves consumption to higher-carbon periods, the mitigation benefit can be small or ambiguous; nevertheless the mechanism’s intended and most common dominant climate effect remains emissions reduction via cleaner dispatch.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy implementing demand response to increase flexibility is fundamentally aimed at altering dispatch and renewable utilization; this general mechanism most consistently maps to mitigation via reduced supply-side emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a given policy’s primary impact is peak shaving, renewable integration, capacity deferral, or cost savings depends on local grid mix/market design and should be assigned at the policy level with supporting evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Benefits depend on marginal emissions factors and program design; poorly designed shifting can increase emissions, and in very low-carbon grids the dominant effect may shift toward reliability/cost rather than GHG reduction.
key_indicators: “Demand response of electricity and heating will be piloted”; “in the City’s premises”; “Based on the pilots… will be estimated”; “a plan will be made for the extent and schedule of the adoption”
co_benefits: Potential cost savings on energy bills; improved grid reliability/peak management; possible local air quality improvements
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted partly as an infrastructure-investment (if pilots require BMS/controls upgrades), but the excerpt emphasizes piloting and developing an adoption plan rather than specifying capital procurement or incentives.
parent_policy_name: Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City (key actions related to the service buildings of the City).

section_header: 4.2.1 Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City
policy_statement: Examine property-specific/regional energy storage opportunities and cost effects, set objectives for storage use (demand response and reserve power), and prepare an operating plan.
parent_statement: Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City (key actions related to the service buildings of the City).
verbatim_text: 36. Property-specific or regional opportunities for storing energy (electricity and heat) will be examined, as will the cost effects ... Based on the results, objectives will be set ... and an operating plan will be prepared.
extraction_rationale: Specific study leading to objectives and an operating plan.
mechanism_description: The policy will examine electricity and heat storage opportunities and costs, then set storage-use objectives and prepare an operating plan to support demand response and reserve power, improving grid flexibility and enabling renewable integration.
primary_causal_pathway: Grid operation/infrastructure change → ↑ flexibility & renewable integration (↓ curtailment, ↓ peakers) → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Utilities, cities, and building/campus operators deploy electrical storage (batteries/thermal storage) and enable interconnection (distribution upgrades, microgrids) to shift load and provide ancillary services.  
2) Mediating physical process: Storage absorbs electricity when supply is abundant (often renewable) and discharges during higher-demand periods, providing ramping/firming, frequency/voltage support, and reserves; this reduces renewable curtailment and decreases reliance on inefficient, high-emitting marginal generators (often gas peakers).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower grid emissions intensity and total power-sector GHG emissions due to increased renewable utilization and reduced fossil generation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling higher renewable electricity penetration (and less fossil peaking/curtailment) through added grid flexibility, thereby reducing GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Storage/interconnection primarily functions to integrate renewables and reduce fossil marginal generation, yielding emissions reductions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While storage can also improve reliability/resilience (adaptation co-benefit) and improve system efficiency (resource efficiency co-benefit), the mechanism as stated centers on renewable integration and grid flexibility—i.e., decarbonizing electricity supply.  
3) Edge cases applied: If storage is charged predominantly from fossil-heavy grids or used mainly for backup diesel displacement/critical power without increasing renewable utilization, mitigation benefits could be smaller or mixed; nevertheless, the canonical mechanism defined here is explicitly “grid flexibility and renewable integration,” which is mitigation-dominant.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is deploying storage to increase flexibility for higher renewable penetration has its largest climate effect through reduced fossil generation and avoided GHG emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a given policy is better characterized secondarily as enabling renewables, peak shaving, ancillary services, microgrids/resilience, or demand response depends on policy-specific design and grid context and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Storage can be adaptation-relevant when framed as backup/resilience (e.g., outage mitigation via microgrids), and emissions impacts depend on charging source and dispatch rules; however, under this mechanism’s definition (renewable integration via flexibility), mitigation remains the dominant category.
key_indicators: "opportunities for storing energy (electricity and heat) will be examined"; "cost effects"; "objectives will be set"; "an operating plan will be prepared"
co_benefits: Energy resilience/backup power (reserve power), potential cost savings/peak shaving, improved reliability
instance_edge_case_notes: Although it includes "objectives will be set," the statement does not specify binding quantitative targets or procurement requirements; therefore classified as planning-strategy rather than target-setting or procurement/investment.
parent_policy_name: Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City (key actions related to the service buildings of the City).

section_header: 4.2.1 Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City
policy_statement: Prepare a target programme for using renewable energy in City construction projects and existing buildings and monitor progress.
parent_statement: Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City (key actions related to the service buildings of the City).
verbatim_text: 37. A target programme for using renewable energy in the City's construction projects and existing buildings will be prepared, and the progress of the programme will be monitored.
extraction_rationale: Plan creation plus monitoring commitment.
mechanism_description: The City will prepare a target programme to increase renewable energy use in construction projects and existing buildings and will monitor progress, which helps displace fossil energy use.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy supply mix shift → ↓ fossil fuel combustion for heat/electricity → ↓ CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: City-led programmes/targets increase renewable energy integration in new construction and existing buildings (e.g., solar PV/solar thermal, renewable district heating, renewable electricity procurement). (2) Process: Renewable generation or renewable-sourced heat/electricity substitutes for fossil-based energy in building operations, lowering combustion and upstream fossil generation. (3) Outcome: Reduced operational CO₂e emissions from buildings (and associated grid/district energy), contributing to climate change mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from building energy use by replacing fossil electricity/heat with renewable energy.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Increasing renewable energy use in buildings primarily works by displacing fossil energy and cutting GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although programs can include efficiency or cost-saving elements, the defining mechanism is fuel switching/supply decarbonization, which is a direct mitigation pathway rather than “same service → less energy input” (Resource Efficiency) or hazard risk reduction (Adaptation).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program were purely about reducing energy demand (without renewables) it would shift toward Resource Efficiency; if framed as backup power for outages it could include Adaptation co-benefits. Here, the described causal chain is explicitly renewable uptake to displace fossil energy.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy matching “renewable energy program in buildings → fossil energy displacement” has its main climate effect through emissions reductions from cleaner energy supply, so Mitigation applies across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype note: Any further sub-classification (e.g., on-site PV vs renewable district heat vs green power procurement) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level coding.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Programs that merely set targets/monitoring without implementation may have uncertain realized impact, but the intended dominant pathway remains mitigation via fossil displacement; co-benefits (energy security/resilience, air quality) do not change the primary category.
key_indicators: "target programme"; "using renewable energy"; "City's construction projects and existing buildings"; "will be prepared"; "progress ... will be monitored"
co_benefits: None
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a planning/strategy instrument (“programme … will be prepared”), but the explicit “target programme” language makes target-setting the best fit. Monitoring is mentioned but not framed as a standalone disclosure/benchmarking requirement.
parent_policy_name: Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City (key actions related to the service buildings of the City).

section_header: 4.2.1 Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City
policy_statement: Continuously develop procurement criteria and methods in construction and maintenance to reflect ambitious goals on energy efficiency, lifecycle CO2 emissions and environmental impacts.
parent_statement: Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City (key actions related to the service buildings of the City).
verbatim_text: 38. The City will constantly develop its criteria and methods for procurements in construction and maintenance so that they will take City's ambitious goals into account in terms of things such as energy efficiency, lifecycle CO2 emissions and environmental impacts.
extraction_rationale: Ongoing procurement policy improvement with specified criteria areas.
mechanism_description: The City will develop procurement criteria for construction and maintenance that account for energy efficiency and lifecycle CO2, leading to lower lifecycle emissions from purchased construction works and services.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Procurement set → ↓ high-carbon materials/processes + ↑ energy-efficient design → ↓ embodied + operational GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city changes how it buys/commissions construction and maintenance (tender specifications, award criteria, and contractor set) to prioritize low lifecycle CO₂ and high energy efficiency.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Suppliers and project teams substitute lower-carbon materials (e.g., lower-clinker concrete, recycled/biobased materials), reduce material quantities, improve construction methods/logistics, and deliver more energy-efficient assets, lowering fossil energy use and process emissions across the value chain.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced embodied emissions (materials manufacturing + construction) and reduced operational emissions (energy use during the asset life), yielding net GHG reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions from construction projects by requiring low-carbon/energy-efficient options through public procurement.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Procurement criteria directly drive lower lifecycle GHG emissions from construction by changing material/process choices and building energy performance.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve resource efficiency (less material/waste) and sometimes resilience (better building performance), the mechanism’s explicit and largest climate effect is emissions reduction (embodied + operational).  
3) Edge cases applied: If procurement focused solely on circularity without clear GHG intent, Resource Efficiency could dominate; if focused solely on climate resilience standards, Adaptation could dominate. Here, lifecycle CO₂ and energy efficiency indicate mitigation-dominant intent and impact.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that uses green construction procurement criteria aimed at lifecycle CO₂/energy efficiency primarily functions by reducing construction-related GHG emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any secondary categorization (e.g., circular economy co-benefits, air quality, resilience) depends on policy-specific evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “lifecycle CO₂” is used only as a reporting requirement without influencing awards/requirements, actual mitigation impact may be weaker; if criteria prioritize adaptation features (e.g., flood-proofing) over emissions, the dominant pathway could shift.
key_indicators: "develop its criteria and methods for procurements"; "construction and maintenance"; "energy efficiency"; "lifecycle CO2 emissions"; "environmental impacts"; "City's ambitious goals"
co_benefits: Reduced broader environmental impacts (e.g., air pollution, resource use, waste), potential cost savings over asset lifecycles.
instance_edge_case_notes: "Constantly develop criteria and methods" signals an ongoing procurement strategy; while it doesn’t specify binding thresholds, it still functions as a procurement requirement because it explicitly updates tender criteria to reflect energy/lifecycle CO₂ goals.

Time span:

2018-2019

primary_causal_pathway: Same building service (comfort/function) → ↓ energy input (construction/operations/maintenance) → ↓ fuel/electricity use → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human/system activity: Increase capability and adherence among designers, contractors, facility managers, and maintenance staff to apply energy-efficient, low-emission construction, commissioning, and O&M practices (often including lifecycle-aware procurement).  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Better envelopes/systems, correct installation/commissioning, improved controls and maintenance reduce heat losses, equipment inefficiencies, and wasted energy across building operation (and can reduce embodied emissions via material choices).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower delivered energy and upstream generation/combustion needs, resulting in reduced CO₂e emissions over the building lifecycle.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced operational energy demand in buildings, leading to lower combustion and power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Capacity-building → more energy-efficient construction/maintenance → reduced building energy use → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the outcome is emissions reduction (mitigation), the defining mechanism is achieving the same building services with less energy input (efficiency), which maps most directly to Resource Efficiency as the primary category for all policies using this mechanism.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific program primarily targets low-carbon materials/embodied carbon rather than energy use, it could shift toward Mitigation; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly culminates in energy-use reduction, keeping Resource Efficiency dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy framed as capacity/competence building for low-carbon/energy-efficient construction and maintenance is intended to systematically reduce energy demand through improved practices, so the primary category remains Resource Efficiency across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether reductions occur mainly via retrofits vs new build, HVAC optimization, commissioning, procurement rules, or embodied-carbon improvements requires policy-level evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be misclassified if “low-emission construction” is implemented mainly through embodied-carbon material substitution with little effect on operational energy; in that case Mitigation might dominate. Here, the stated endpoint is “energy use reduction,” so Resource Efficiency is appropriate.
key_indicators: “commitment and competence … will be strengthened”; “actors responsible for construction and maintenance”; “low emission levels”; “energy efficiency”; “lifecycle impacts”; “procurement environmental criteria”; “Time span: 2018-2019”
co_benefits: Improved indoor comfort/health, reduced operating costs, potential air-pollution reductions from lower energy use
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be coded as an information/capacity-building instrument (training/guidelines), but the text is framed at a program/strategic level (“will be strengthened”) without specifying a disclosure requirement or a binding standard.

section_header: 4.2.1 Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City

parent_statement: Residential and service buildings and outdoor lighting owned by the City (key actions related to the service buildings of the City).
verbatim_text: 40. Policies, objectives and procedures will be set for facility projects with conflicting interests related to energy efficiency and the utilisation of renewable energy, i.e. costs, profitability, protection values, architectonic solutions and the cityscape.
extraction_rationale: Governance/procedural policy to resolve trade-offs.
mechanism_description: The city will set policies, objectives, and procedures for facility projects to resolve conflicts around energy efficiency and renewable energy so more projects can incorporate these measures and reduce energy-related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance procedures for capital projects → ↑ adoption of energy efficiency + renewables → ↓ fossil energy use/↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: City alters internal policies, objectives, and project review/procurement procedures for municipal facility projects to resolve tradeoffs (e.g., cost, heritage/architecture, cityscape) in favor of incorporating efficiency and on-site/contracted renewable energy.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Improved building envelope/systems and higher-efficiency equipment reduce delivered energy demand; renewable electricity/heat supply substitutes for fossil generation in the grid or district energy mix.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower combustion of fossil fuels (directly on-site and/or upstream in power/heat generation) leading to reduced CO₂e emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from lower energy consumption and increased renewable energy adoption in facilities due to enabling review/procurement/project procedures.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Governance/project-review reforms → more efficiency/renewables in facilities → lower fossil energy use → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can incidentally improve energy/resource efficiency, the dominant end effect is emissions reduction (mitigation) via reduced fossil energy demand/supply, not primarily adaptation (hazard reduction) or nature-based carbon sinks.  
3) Edge cases applied: If procedures were purely administrative without affecting built outcomes, climate impact would be indirect/uncertain; however, the stated intent is explicitly to enable efficiency/renewables adoption, which is mitigation-dominant.  
4) Applies to all instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that modifies project review/procedures to resolve conflicts and enable efficiency/renewables is aimed at increasing these measures and thereby reducing energy-related emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: More specific subtyping (e.g., building efficiency vs on-site solar vs procurement of renewable electricity) requires policy-level details on what measures are prioritized and implemented.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may primarily streamline decision-making or balance heritage/architecture concerns without binding performance requirements; in those cases realized emissions benefits depend on enforcement strength and downstream project uptake, but the dominant intended pathway remains mitigation through efficiency/renewables.
key_indicators: “Policies, objectives and procedures will be set”; “facility projects”; “conflicting interests”; “energy efficiency”; “utilisation of renewable energy”; “costs, profitability, protection values, architectonic solutions and the cityscape”
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: heritage/protection value preservation, improved architectural/cityscape outcomes, potential operating cost savings—mentioned but not framed as separate policy goals beyond resolving trade-offs.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a regulatory/mandate instrument if these procedures are binding approval gates for projects, but the text does not specify mandatory compliance criteria or performance thresholds; “planning-strategy” best fits the procedural/governance framing.

primary_causal_pathway: Construction procurement/design choices → ↓ embodied emissions from material production/transport + construction processes → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Building owners, designers, and contractors shift specifications and procurement toward lower-carbon materials (e.g., low-clinker/LC3 cement, recycled steel, mass timber where appropriate), optimized structural design (material efficiency), and lower-emission construction methods, driven by embodied-carbon caps/targets or procurement rules.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced fossil-fuel combustion and process emissions in industrial material production (notably cement calcination and energy use), plus reduced transport and site emissions; potentially increased use of recycled content reduces primary production demand.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower cradle-to-gate/cradle-to-grave CO2e per m² (or per project) and lower total community-wide GHG emissions associated with the built environment’s capital stock expansion/renewal.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing lifecycle (embodied) GHG emissions from construction materials and processes used in buildings and infrastructure.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Policies using this mechanism primarily act by changing material selection and construction practices to directly reduce upstream and onsite GHG emissions (embodied carbon), which is a mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some measures (e.g., green roofs, high-albedo surfaces) can yield adaptation co-benefits (heat reduction, stormwater), the defining mechanism here is explicit embodied-carbon limits/targets and low-carbon procurement—i.e., emissions reduction rather than hazard/exposure reduction or resource-efficiency-as-end (even if material efficiency contributes).  
3) Edge cases applied:  
   - If a policy emphasizes “material efficiency” (using less material) without explicit GHG framing, it could resemble Resource Efficiency; however, within this canonical mechanism the intent and metric are embodied CO2e reductions, keeping Mitigation primary.  
   - If “nature-based materials” (e.g., timber) are promoted mainly for carbon storage/sinks, it can overlap with Nature-Based Solutions; still, across instances the dominant effect remains reducing net GHG via embodied-carbon reduction requirements.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that sets embodied-carbon limits/targets or systematically drives lower-carbon material procurement has its largest climate effect through reduced lifecycle construction-related emissions, which is mitigation.  
5) Note on secondary subtype: Any secondary classification (e.g., procurement policy vs. building code; inclusion of adaptation co-benefits like cool roofs/green roofs) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on specific text and evidence, and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some representative policies bundle broader “environmental impacts” (e.g., green roofs, permeable/high-albedo pavements) that may primarily deliver adaptation or urban-heat benefits; however, for this canonical mechanism the unifying dominant pathway is embodied-carbon reduction (mitigation). If a policy lacks explicit embodied-carbon accounting/metrics and focuses mainly on heat/stormwater, it may not truly belong in this mechanism family.

co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: reduced local air pollutants from construction/material supply chains; innovation/market transformation in low-carbon materials; improved cost control via material efficiency. (Not stated explicitly as objectives.)

mechanism_description: The City will pilot energy-plus building construction and develop a plan to expand it, leading to lower net building energy use and associated emissions.

primary_causal_pathway: Building design/retrofit standards → ↓ delivered energy demand + ↓ on-site fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing

(1) Change in human activity/system: set for new construction and/or major renovations (and sometimes existing-building performance standards) that raise minimum efficiency, tighten envelopes, improve HVAC/lighting/water-heating performance, and may set electrification or low-carbon heat.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced heat transfer losses, improved system efficiencies (higher COP/AFUE equivalents), better controls and reduced peak loads; reduced combustion of natural gas/oil on-site; lower electricity demand from the grid.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower operational (Scope 1 and 2) energy-related GHG emissions over the building lifetime; in net-zero/energy-plus cases, near-zero or net-negative operational emissions depending on grid intensity and on-site generation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building operational energy use (and fuel switching away from fossil combustion), which lowers GHG emissions from energy supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: These policies act on building energy performance so the largest effect is lowering operational energy consumption and fossil fuel use, which directly reduces GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While efficiency also improves “resource efficiency,” the climate-dominant purpose and outcome here is emissions reduction from energy use; therefore Mitigation best matches the primary causal pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a code is framed around resilience (e.g., passive survivability during outages/heatwaves) it could have an adaptation co-benefit, but the canonical mechanism explicitly targets energy/emissions reduction, keeping Mitigation primary.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy under “building_energy_code → energy_use_reduction” fundamentally reduces operational energy demand and/or on-site combustion, yielding emissions reductions regardless of city or code stringency.  
5) Note on subtypes: Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., “efficiency,” “electrification,” “net-zero,” “performance standard”) is deferred to policy-level evidence and specific provisions.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some building-code policies can include adaptation-focused elements (cool roofs for heat, flood-proofing, backup power) or embodied-carbon limits; those would introduce additional pathways, but under this canonical mechanism the dominant pathway remains operational energy/emissions reduction (Mitigation).
key_indicators: "pilot energy-plus construction"; "examine the cost effects and challenges"; "prepare a plan for moving gradually towards energy-plus construction"
co_benefits: Reduced energy bills; improved indoor comfort/health; local job creation in high-performance construction
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an incentive-program if the “pilot” involves subsidies or developer incentives, but the text does not specify financial support or requirements—only piloting, assessment, and planning—so classified as planning-strategy.

policy_statement: Prepare actions and instructions to increase use of recycled materials in housing construction, infrastructure construction and maintenance by identifying obstacles and opportunities.
parent_statement: Circular economy in construction projects (key actions related to circular economy in construction projects).
verbatim_text: 44. The obstacles and opportunities related to increasing the proportion of recycled materials in housing construction, infrastructural construction and maintenance. Actions and instructions for this will be prepared.
extraction_rationale: Commitment to develop guidance and actions to increase recycled materials.
mechanism_description: It prepares actions and instructions to increase recycled material use in housing and infrastructure construction and maintenance, reducing emissions from producing and transporting virgin construction materials.
primary_causal_pathway: Material sourcing change (↑ recycled inputs) → ↓ virgin material production/transport → ↓ embodied GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed activity/system: Construction and infrastructure projects shift procurement/specifications from virgin materials to recycled/secondary materials (and design for reuse where relevant).  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced upstream industrial production (and sometimes avoided landfill/incineration) lowers energy use and process emissions in material manufacturing (e.g., clinker production, primary steelmaking) and reduces freight demand for raw materials.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower embodied (scope 3/upstream) GHG emissions per unit of built area/infrastructure delivered, contributing to overall emissions reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle (embodied) GHG emissions from avoided production and transport of virgin construction materials.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: substituting recycled for virgin construction materials reduces upstream industrial energy/process emissions and transport—i.e., a direct GHG mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve material efficiency (a resource-efficiency co-benefit), the climate-dominant effect described is emissions reduction, not merely reduced material throughput or adaptation/resilience outcomes.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy’s main intent were waste diversion or circularity without clear GHG claims, classification could drift toward Resource Efficiency; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly ties recycled materials to embodied emissions reduction, keeping Mitigation dominant.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that increases recycled material use specifically to reduce embodied emissions shares the same primary mitigation pathway via avoided virgin production/transport emissions.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any finer subtype (e.g., industrial decarbonization vs. circular economy, scope attribution, or whether landfill methane avoidance is material) must be assigned based on policy-specific evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some recycled-material pathways can increase emissions if recycling is highly energy-intensive or causes longer transport distances; also, benefits may partly come from avoided waste disposal. These do not change the dominant intended pathway for this canonical mechanism unless policy evidence indicates the primary goal/effect is waste management rather than embodied-carbon mitigation.
key_indicators: “obstacles and opportunities”; “Actions and instructions … will be prepared”; “increasing the proportion of recycled materials in housing construction, infrastructural construction and maintenance”
co_benefits: Reduced waste to landfill/incineration; resource conservation; potential cost savings and local circular-economy job creation
instance_edge_case_notes: Could potentially be interpreted as a precursor to regulatory or procurement requirements, but the wording limits it to preparation of guidance/instructions rather than mandating recycled content or allocating funding.

policy_statement: Implement principles and a 2018–2021 action plan for using excavated earth, rock and demolition material in groundwork, and participate in developing/adopting the regional landmass GIS tool (SeutuMaisa).
parent_statement: Circular economy in construction projects (key actions related to circular economy in construction projects).

The City will participate in the development and adoption of the regional landmass tool using geographical information (SeutuMaisa).
extraction_rationale: Time-bound implementation of an action plan and participation in a specific tool rollout.
mechanism_description: Putting into practice principles for using excavated earth, rock, and demolition materials in groundwork, supported by a regional landmass GIS tool, increases material reuse and reduces emissions from producing and transporting virgin construction materials.
primary_causal_pathway: Construction material sourcing practices → ↓ virgin material production + ↓ material transport → ↓ lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Construction and earthworks shift from disposing excavated earth/rock and demolition materials (and purchasing virgin aggregates/fill) to reusing these materials on-site or within the region, enabled by planning principles and a GIS “materials exchange/stock” tool.  
(2) Mediating process: Reuse displaces extraction and processing of virgin aggregates and reduces hauling distances and disposal needs, cutting fuel combustion and industrial process energy in material production.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower embodied CO₂e from construction projects (materials + transport), yielding net GHG emissions reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced embodied GHG emissions from avoided production and transport of virgin construction materials due to increased reuse of excavated and demolition materials.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Increased reuse of excavated/demolition materials primarily reduces upstream and transport-related emissions from virgin material supply chains (embodied carbon).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the policy improves resource efficiency (less waste, less raw material throughput), the principal climate-relevant impact across instances is the resulting reduction in CO₂e, which is a mitigation outcome; adaptation benefits (e.g., resilience) are not the main causal chain described.  
3) Edge cases applied: If reuse leads to significant additional processing, long-distance hauling, or quality constraints that negate emissions savings, the mitigation benefit could be smaller; however, the canonical mechanism’s intent and typical implementation target net emissions reduction.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism (construction material reuse substituting virgin production/transport) has its dominant climate effect as embodied-emissions reduction, even if co-benefits include waste reduction or cost savings.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any secondary classification (e.g., “circular economy/resource efficiency co-benefit,” “procurement,” “waste diversion”) is deferred to policy-specific evidence and is not set at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential rebound/negation if reused materials require energy-intensive processing, contamination treatment, or longer transport than virgin supply; some implementations may be framed as “resource efficiency,” but the dominant climate effect remains embodied GHG mitigation.
key_indicators: “principles”; “action plan (2018-2021) will be put into practice”; “participate in the development and adoption”; “regional landmass tool using geographical information (SeutuMaisa)”; “using excavated earth, rock material and demolition material in groundwork”
co_benefits: Reduced landfill/disposal volumes; reduced local truck traffic and associated air pollution/noise; resource conservation (less aggregate extraction).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be construed as an information-disclosure/data-platform instrument due to the GIS tool, but the core commitment is an action plan/principles (planning strategy) rather than a standalone disclosure requirement or a binding procurement/permitting mandate.

causal_mechanism_detail:

2) Mediating physical process: Thermal energy that would be expelled to ambient air or lost in wastewater is captured and transferred to incoming ventilation air or a heat distribution loop, raising the effective coefficient of performance and reducing set primary heating input.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower final energy demand for space heating (and sometimes domestic hot water), which reduces upstream energy production and associated GHG emissions (and can also reduce peak heating load).

2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the ultimate climate benefit is mitigation (↓ GHG), the defining, first-order mechanism is reduced energy requirement for the same service—i.e., resource/energy efficiency—rather than direct fuel switching or carbon sequestration.  
3) Edge cases applied: If recovered heat is driven by high-carbon electricity for heat pumps or causes significant refrigerant leakage, net mitigation benefits could be reduced, but the mechanism remains an efficiency improvement. If deployed mainly for cooling/comfort resilience, there could be adaptation co-benefits, but not the dominant effect described.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy implementing exhaust-air or sewage heat recovery for buildings shares the same primary pathway of lowering delivered heating energy via waste-heat capture, so Resource Efficiency is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific mitigation magnitude, electrification interactions, refrigerant management, and whether it is tied to district heating or building-scale systems should be assigned based on policy-level technical details and local grid/heat carbon intensity.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net GHG impact depends on the carbon intensity of electricity (for heat pumps), system design/commissioning, and refrigerant leakage; in rare cases with very low-carbon heat supply already, the dominant benefit may be cost/peak-load reduction more than emissions, but the mechanism remains energy efficiency via heat recovery.

primary_causal_pathway: Renewable electricity deployment → fossil generation displacement → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Electricity supply mix shifts as PV capacity and generation increase (utility-scale, rooftop, or community solar), changing dispatch and reducing fossil plant run-hours and output.  
2) Mediating process: PV produces electricity without on-site combustion; on the grid, this zero/low-operational-emissions generation substitutes for marginal fossil generation (often gas or coal), reducing fossil fuel use and associated CO2 (and some CH4/N2O) emissions; effects depend on marginal emissions factors, curtailment, and storage/transmission constraints.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in power-sector GHG emissions (tCO2e) and, secondarily, reductions in co-pollutants from fossil generation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is that added solar PV generation displaces fossil electricity generation, lowering power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: PV deployment increases renewable generation that substitutes for fossil generation, yielding a direct decrease in combustion-related GHG emissions—this is classic emissions mitigation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While PV can improve resource efficiency (less fuel input per kWh) and can support resilience if paired with storage/islanding, the principal and most general effect across all such policies is emissions reduction via fossil displacement.  
3) Edge cases applied: If PV is added in a grid with high curtailment or already near-zero marginal emissions, emissions benefits may be smaller, but the intended and typical dominant pathway remains fossil displacement and mitigation. Lifecycle/manufacturing emissions do not overturn the primary operational mitigation effect in standard accounting.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that materially deploys solar PV to increase electricity production shares this dominant mitigation pathway because the mechanism explicitly relies on renewable generation displacing fossil generation.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any further subtype/secondary tagging (e.g., rooftop vs utility-scale, community ownership, resilience microgrids, equity components) is deferred to policy-level evidence and does not change the primary category.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions impact depends on the marginal grid mix, curtailment, and whether PV is paired with storage; PV deployed primarily for backup power/resilience could introduce an adaptation co-benefit, but the canonical mechanism specified is fossil generation displacement (mitigation).

63. Pre-emptive guidance for housing companies ... will be developed to promote energy-efficient renovations and the use of renewable energy sources.

- ... the implementation instructions for the geothermal heating project ... will be taken into use... Additionally, the opportunity to exempt geothermal heating projects from building control licence fees will be explored.
extraction_rationale: Concrete guidance development and specific administrative change exploration.
mechanism_description: The city will develop guidance, take geothermal implementation instructions into use, and explore fee exemptions to encourage geothermal and other renewable energy use in housing companies, reducing fossil-based heating.
primary_causal_pathway: Renewable heat deployment → fossil fuel combustion displacement → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Building owners/housing companies and public facilities switch from fossil-fired boilers/district heat inputs to renewable heating systems (e.g., geothermal heat, solar thermal for hot water). (2) Mediating process: Renewable heat provides thermal energy with little-to-no on-site combustion; increased renewable share reduces demand for fossil fuels in heating supply chains. (3) Outcome: Reduced CO₂ (and potentially methane) emissions associated with space and water heating energy use.
dominant_pathway_test: Deploying renewable heat systems displaces fossil-based heating energy, leading to lower GHG emissions from building heat supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Renewable heating is installed and used, which directly displaces fossil heating, reducing GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While these policies can also improve energy efficiency or resilience, the mechanism specified is specifically “renewable heating deployment → fossil heat displacement,” whose largest climate-relevant effect is emissions mitigation from fuel switching.  
3) Edge cases applied: If renewable heat is powered by high-carbon electricity (e.g., some heat pumps on fossil-heavy grids) the benefit may be reduced, but the mechanism’s intent and typical effect remains fossil heat displacement; similarly, efficiency co-benefits do not override the primary mitigation pathway.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism—whether guidance/fee exemptions for geothermal or installing solar water heaters—operates by increasing renewable heat supply to replace fossil heat, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Note on secondary subtype: Any further sub-classification (e.g., demand-side efficiency co-benefits, cost-saving/resource-efficiency aspects, or adaptation co-benefits) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on evidence and context.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions impact depends on what fossil heat is displaced and the lifecycle/operational carbon intensity of the renewable system (e.g., electricity mix for heat pumps, geothermal drilling impacts, embodied emissions). If the “renewable” system primarily reduces energy use without clear fossil displacement, some instances could trend toward Resource Efficiency, but that is outside this canonical mechanism as defined.

co_benefits: Lower household energy costs; improved indoor comfort/health; reduced local air pollution

verbatim_text: 69. The 3D model (the Energy and Climate Atlas) will be developed in the energy efficiency guidance for residents. Energy tools for the assessment of the costs and potential improvements in buildings' energy consumption will be developed.
extraction_rationale: Specific tool development commitment for guidance.
mechanism_description: The city will develop an Energy and Climate Atlas and building energy assessment tools to guide residents and inform cost-effective improvements that can reduce building energy use.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Information transparency/market signal → efficiency actions (O&M + retrofits) → ↓ building energy use → ↓ fossil energy combustion → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Cities set or facilitate energy/water benchmarking, monitoring, and/or public reporting so owners, tenants, lenders, and regulators can compare performance and identify inefficiencies.  
(2) Mediating physical/process link: Improved data availability and comparability triggers operational improvements (commissioning, controls tuning), capital upgrades (HVAC, envelope, lighting), and behavioral changes; these measures reduce kWh and on-site fuel consumption for the same building service levels.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower energy demand reduces upstream grid emissions and direct combustion emissions, decreasing overall GHG emissions from the buildings sector.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption leading to lower GHG emissions from electricity generation and on-site fuel use.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: transparency and benchmarking → motivates/identifies efficiency improvements → lowers energy use → lowers GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while water data and energy-burden reporting can support equity and resource efficiency, the core mechanism is to drive building energy efficiency, with the main climate impact being emissions reduction (mitigation).  
3) Edge cases applied: if disclosure is used purely for equity planning or billing assistance without driving efficiency actions, climate impact could be indirect; however, the canonical mechanism specified is explicitly “market signal for efficiency,” keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy that expands benchmarking/monitoring/disclosure to create performance comparisons and motivate efficiency improvements shares the same primary mitigation pathway through reduced building energy use.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether outcomes come mainly from operational changes vs retrofits, and whether electricity vs gas dominates, must be determined at the individual policy level with implementation details and local energy mix.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some related policies emphasize water benchmarking or “energy burden” reporting; those can be primarily equity/resource-planning tools unless explicitly tied to efficiency interventions. Municipal-only monitoring may yield smaller market signaling effects but still targets efficiency-driven emissions reductions.
key_indicators: “3D model (the Energy and Climate Atlas)”; “developed in the energy efficiency guidance for residents”; “Energy tools for the assessment of the costs and potential improvements”; “buildings' energy consumption”
co_benefits: Potential household cost savings/energy affordability benefits; improved comfort/health from efficiency upgrades
instance_edge_case_notes: Although framed as “guidance” rather than formal “public disclosure,” the core instrument is still information provision/decision-support (atlas/tools) rather than a mandate, pricing, or direct investment.

policy_statement: Improve energy-related competence of housing cooperative decision-makers through training and case presentations on energy efficiency and renewable energy.
parent_statement: Key actions related to the energy renaissance.
verbatim_text:
70. The energy-related competences of the decision-makers in housing cooperatives will be improved.

- Decision-makers of housing cooperatives will receive training ...
- Case presentations ... will be prepared.
extraction_rationale: Concrete capacity-building actions specified.
mechanism_description: Training and case presentations improve housing cooperative decision-makers’ competence on energy efficiency and renewable energy, helping enable choices that reduce building energy use and increase clean energy uptake.
primary_causal_pathway: Capacity building/training → ↑ adoption of efficiency + renewables → ↓ fossil energy use in buildings → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changes: Improves the competence and decision processes of housing cooperative boards/decision-makers regarding energy efficiency measures and renewable energy procurement/installation.  
(2) Mediating process: Better-informed decisions overcome informational/transaction-cost barriers, leading to higher uptake and quality of retrofits (insulation, controls, HVAC upgrades) and clean energy (e.g., solar PV, renewable electricity contracts), reducing delivered energy and carbon intensity.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower operational building energy demand and/or cleaner energy supply, producing net reductions in CO₂e emissions from building operations.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption and associated combustion-related GHG emissions due to more frequent/better energy-efficiency and clean-energy investment decisions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Training/capacity-building changes decisions → increases adoption of efficiency/renewables → reduces fossil energy use and thus GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The primary intended and largest climate effect is emissions reduction, not adaptation (reducing hazard exposure) or resource efficiency as an end in itself; efficiency here is a means to cut energy-related emissions in buildings.  
3) Edge cases applied: If training leads mainly to energy cost savings without measurable emissions impact, it still primarily targets emissions via energy reduction; if it focused on resilience (e.g., backup power for outages), it could shift toward Adaptation, but that is not indicated in this mechanism description.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy using this mechanism—training building decision-makers to enable efficiency/renewables choices—primarily drives operational emissions reductions in the buildings sector.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the downstream impact is mostly efficiency vs renewables, and the magnitude, depends on policy design and uptake evidence at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the training is primarily about comfort/health or resilience (e.g., overheating preparedness, outage planning) with minimal energy/emissions effects, classification could shift; similarly, if it is purely “market development” without clear link to emissions outcomes, confidence would be lower. Otherwise straightforward.
key_indicators: “competences … will be improved”; “will receive training”; “Case presentations … will be prepared”; “energy efficiency”; “renewable energy”
co_benefits: Lower energy bills/operating costs; improved indoor comfort; potential health benefits from better building performance
instance_edge_case_notes: Classified as information-disclosure because the primary lever is training/case-study knowledge transfer; could also be seen as a planning-strategy/capacity-building program, but no formal plan, targets, or investment components are specified.
parent_policy_name: Key actions related to the energy renaissance.

policy_statement: Key actions related to new energy solutions.
verbatim_text: ## 4.2.4 New energy solutions
extraction_rationale: Introduces a numbered list of actions (71–82) under a named section.
mechanism_description: The document references “new energy solutions,” indicating an intent to pursue changes in energy approaches that support an overall emissions reduction pathway.
primary_causal_pathway: Policy target-setting + governance → cross-sector decarbonization actions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city adopts a binding or guiding community-wide emissions target (e.g., % reduction by a year, net-zero), aligning planning, budgeting, regulations, and investments across energy, buildings, transport, waste, and industry.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels, improved energy efficiency, fuel switching/electrification, methane reductions, and other sector measures lower the mass flow of GHGs released to the atmosphere.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower community-wide GHG emissions (CO₂e) leading to reduced cumulative atmospheric GHG concentrations and reduced radiative forcing over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is achieving economy-wide reductions in greenhouse gas emissions (often to net-zero) across major sectors.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s central function is to set an economy-wide emissions outcome that triggers and coordinates decarbonization measures, yielding ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While targets can indirectly improve efficiency or resilience, their defining and largest intended climate effect is emissions mitigation at the community scale, not adaptation (hazard/exposure reduction), not resource efficiency as an end in itself, and not ecosystem-based carbon sequestration as the primary lever.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some targets include “net-zero” via offsets or land-sector sinks; even then, the dominant climate-relevant purpose remains achieving net emissions reductions/neutrality (mitigation). If a document is purely aspirational with no implementation linkage, it is still categorized by intended dominant pathway (mitigation).  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that sets a community-wide GHG reduction or net-zero target shares the same primary mechanism—economy-wide emissions reduction—regardless of sector mix or local context.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether delivery relies more on efficiency, electrification, renewables, waste methane controls, offsets, or nature-based sinks requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Targets framed mainly as “climate neutrality” can be met with offsets or external credits; classification remains Mitigation, but downstream interpretation should note whether reductions are in-boundary vs. compensated. Targets limited to municipal operations (not community-wide) would be a different mechanism and should not be conflated.
key_indicators: “New energy solutions”
co_benefits: None
instance_edge_case_notes: Classification is underdetermined because the verbatim text contains no actionable policy language (no targets, mandates, funding, standards, or programs). “planning-strategy” is inferred from the section header context rather than explicitly stated.

policy_statement: Helen will implement customer-end emissions reduction actions including improving district heating connection measurements and tariff adjustments, examining hourly district heating tariffs, and developing energy monitoring interfaces for customers.
parent_statement: Key actions related to new energy solutions.
verbatim_text:
71. Helen Ltd will implement emissions reduction actions based on its own development programme and decisions. The actions include:

- Continuing the inspection of the measurements of the properties' district heating connections and changing the tariffs for properties to match the more accurate measurements.
- The cost and incentive effects of a hourly tariff for district heating will be examined...
- Opportunities for monitoring energy consumption ... will be developed by offering the client an open interface...
extraction_rationale: Concrete actions and studies by a named utility actor.
mechanism_description: Helen will improve district heating connection measurements, adjust (and examine hourly) tariffs, and develop customer energy monitoring interfaces to create better information and price incentives that reduce district heating energy use and related emissions.
typology_evidence_quote: improving district heating connection measurements and tariff adjustments, examining hourly district heating tariffs, and developing energy monitoring interfaces for customers
primary_causal_pathway: Information + price signals → ↓ heat demand → ↓ fuel combustion for heat production → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Improve metering accuracy/granularity, introduce hourly/dynamic tariffs, and provide customer energy monitoring interfaces to influence end-user heat consumption and enable better control/awareness.  
(2) Mediating process: Price elasticity and feedback effects drive conservation and load shifting; lower aggregate heat load reduces heat generation set and upstream fuel input (and may improve plant dispatch/efficiency).  
(3) Outcome: Reduced combustion-related CO₂ (and potentially CH₄/N₂O depending on fuel mix) from district heating production, yielding net GHG mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced district heating demand, which lowers fuel combustion at heat plants and therefore reduces GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Metering + dynamic pricing changes consumption behavior → lower district heat demand → less heat generation and fuel combustion → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the mechanism can improve energy productivity (a resource-efficiency framing), the climate-relevant endpoint emphasized and most consequential is the reduction in combustion emissions from heat production, which is mitigation. It is not primarily adaptation (no hazard/exposure pathway) or nature-based solutions (no ecosystem sink pathway).  
3) Edge cases applied: If the district heating system were already near-zero-carbon (e.g., fully renewable/waste heat with minimal marginal emissions), the dominant climate effect could shift toward resource efficiency/cost savings; however, for the mechanism as defined (“related emissions” reduction from lower heat use), the general dominant pathway remains emissions mitigation.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that uses metering + dynamic pricing to reduce district heat demand will, in typical systems, reduce required heat generation and thus GHG emissions; therefore Mitigation is the consistent primary category for this canonical mechanism.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: The exact mitigation subtype (e.g., demand-side efficiency vs. demand response vs. pricing reform) and quantified impact depend on policy-specific evidence (fuel mix, marginal emissions, tariff structure), and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If marginal district heat is supplied by low/zero-carbon sources (e.g., surplus renewable heat, geothermal, large heat pumps on zero-carbon electricity, or waste heat that would otherwise be rejected), the emissions reduction per unit of heat saved may be small and the dominant framing could lean toward Resource Efficiency; nevertheless the canonical mechanism’s intended and typical climate impact is mitigation via reduced heat production emissions.
key_indicators: “changing the tariffs”; “hourly tariff for district heating will be examined”; “incentive effects”; “inspection of the measurements”; “monitoring energy consumption”; “offering the client an open interface”
co_benefits: Improved billing accuracy and fairness; potential customer cost savings; better demand management and peak load reduction; improved customer engagement/service quality.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes both pricing (tariffs) and information/feedback (monitoring interface). Classified by the primary lever (tariff design) as a pricing mechanism; the interface component could alternatively be coded as information-disclosure if treated as the main instrument.
secondary_gate_reason: contextual_pass: quote matched secondary token 'heat'; row context matched primary token 'emission'
parent_policy_name: Key actions related to new energy solutions.

policy_statement: Identify and schedule renewable district heating procurement for the City organisation and create a plan to reach 100% renewable district heating.
parent_statement: Key actions related to new energy solutions.
verbatim_text: 72. The opportunities to acquire renewable district heating will be identified in the City organisation, as will the cost effects thereof. The acquisition will be scheduled. A plan will be created for the acquisition of renewable district heating and on the date when its proportion will reach 100 per cent of all district heating.
extraction_rationale: Clear procurement commitment with 100% endpoint planning.
mechanism_description: The City organisation will identify and schedule acquisition of renewable district heating and create a plan to increase its share to 100%, displacing fossil-based district heating and lowering emissions.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Renewable heat deployment → fossil fuel combustion displacement → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Building owners/housing companies and public facilities switch from fossil-fired boilers/district heat inputs to renewable heating systems (e.g., geothermal heat, solar thermal for hot water). (2) Mediating process: Renewable heat provides thermal energy with little-to-no on-site combustion; increased renewable share reduces demand for fossil fuels in heating supply chains. (3) Outcome: Reduced CO₂ (and potentially methane) emissions associated with space and water heating energy use.
dominant_pathway_test: Deploying renewable heat systems displaces fossil-based heating energy, leading to lower GHG emissions from building heat supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Renewable heating is installed and used, which directly displaces fossil heating, reducing GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While these policies can also improve energy efficiency or resilience, the mechanism specified is specifically “renewable heating deployment → fossil heat displacement,” whose largest climate-relevant effect is emissions mitigation from fuel switching.  
3) Edge cases applied: If renewable heat is powered by high-carbon electricity (e.g., some heat pumps on fossil-heavy grids) the benefit may be reduced, but the mechanism’s intent and typical effect remains fossil heat displacement; similarly, efficiency co-benefits do not override the primary mitigation pathway.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism—whether guidance/fee exemptions for geothermal or installing solar water heaters—operates by increasing renewable heat supply to replace fossil heat, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Note on secondary subtype: Any further sub-classification (e.g., demand-side efficiency co-benefits, cost-saving/resource-efficiency aspects, or adaptation co-benefits) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on evidence and context.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions impact depends on what fossil heat is displaced and the lifecycle/operational carbon intensity of the renewable system (e.g., electricity mix for heat pumps, geothermal drilling impacts, embodied emissions). If the “renewable” system primarily reduces energy use without clear fossil displacement, some instances could trend toward Resource Efficiency, but that is outside this canonical mechanism as defined.
key_indicators: “acquire renewable district heating”; “The acquisition will be scheduled”; “A plan will be created”; “when its proportion will reach 100 per cent of all district heating”; “cost effects”
co_benefits: Potential air quality improvements and reduced exposure to fossil fuel price volatility (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be read as a planning/target-setting instrument (“plan… reach 100%”), but because it centers on “acquisition” within the City organisation, procurement-requirement is the best fit.
parent_policy_name: Key actions related to new energy solutions.
