parent_statement: Key actions related to new energy solutions.
verbatim_text: 73. The City will acquire renewable/emission-free electricity. Purchasing units will prepare a scheduled plan for the switch to renewable electricity.
extraction_rationale: Concrete procurement commitment plus required implementation planning.
mechanism_description: The City will acquire renewable/emission-free electricity and prepare plans to switch purchasing units to renewables, increasing clean power procurement and displacing fossil electricity generation.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Electricity procurement / demand shift → ↑ renewable generation share → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city changes electricity sourcing for public buildings (and sometimes broader municipal operations) to purchase/contract for renewable electricity rather than conventional grid mix.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Increased renewable electricity supply (via PPAs, RECs, utility green tariffs, or on-/off-site projects) reduces the need for fossil-fueled power plants to generate electricity, lowering combustion-related emissions (and associated upstream fuel-cycle emissions).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in CO₂e emissions from electricity generation attributable to municipal electricity consumption, contributing to overall grid decarbonization and lower cumulative emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing GHG emissions by replacing fossil electricity generation with renewable electricity on the grid.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The core causal chain is “procure more renewable electricity → displace fossil generation → reduce GHG emissions,” which is directly mitigation-focused.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While renewable procurement can incidentally improve energy security or price stability (resource efficiency/resilience co-benefits), the dominant and intended climate effect is emissions reduction from the power sector.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy only claims “renewable” via unbundled certificates with weak additionality, real-world emissions impacts can be smaller; however, the mechanism as stated still targets grid decarbonization via renewable sourcing and is best categorized as Mitigation.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that increases renewable electricity procurement/use (especially for municipal/public buildings) operates through the same primary pathway of reducing fossil-based electricity emissions.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any secondary subtype distinctions (e.g., municipal operations vs. community-wide targets; on-site generation vs. procurement instruments; REC quality/additionality) require policy-specific evidence and are deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions benefits depend on additionality and accounting (e.g., bundled vs. unbundled RECs, local vs. remote procurement, marginal grid emissions). Some “clean energy” targets may include nuclear or fossil w/ CCS; classification remains Mitigation because the dominant intended pathway is reduced power-sector GHG emissions.
key_indicators: "will acquire renewable/emission-free electricity"; "purchase renewable/emission-free electricity"; "Purchasing units will prepare a scheduled plan"; "switch to renewable electricity"
co_benefits: None
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a combination of procurement-requirement (buy clean electricity) plus planning-strategy (scheduled plan). Classified primarily as procurement-requirement because the binding action is purchasing renewable/emission-free electricity; the plan is an implementation aid.
parent_policy_name: Key actions related to new energy solutions.

policy_statement: Automate and make water consumption measurement hour-based and remotely readable; HSY recommends consumption-based invoicing.
parent_statement: Key actions related to new energy solutions.
verbatim_text: 74. The primary measurement of water consumption will be automated and made hour-based and remotely readable. HSY recommends consumption-based invoicing.
extraction_rationale: Specific metering modernization action; relevant to energy/water use.
mechanism_description: Automating hourly, remotely readable water metering and recommending consumption-based invoicing improves consumption visibility and price signals, supporting reduced water use.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Same water service → ↓ water demand → ↓ energy for pumping/treatment/heating → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Deploy hourly, remotely readable smart meters and pair them with consumption feedback and/or consumption-based pricing/billing to influence user behavior and utility operations (e.g., leak detection, demand management). (2) Mediating process: Improved visibility and price signals reduce discretionary use and accelerate leak identification/repair, lowering total volumes conveyed and treated. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced electricity/heat set for abstraction, treatment, distribution, wastewater collection/treatment, and in many cases reduced end-use hot-water heating, yielding lower GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced energy use (and associated GHG emissions) across water supply/wastewater systems—and often hot-water heating—due to lower per-capita water consumption.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism reduces the quantity of water demanded via measurement/feedback and pricing signals, which in turn reduces energy use in water and wastewater systems (and potentially hot-water heating), lowering emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there is a mitigation co-benefit (↓ GHG), the core mechanism is delivering the same service (water supply) with less resource throughput—i.e., efficiency—so Resource Efficiency best captures the dominant pathway common to all such policies.  
3) Edge cases applied: In drought-prone contexts, the same mechanism can also support adaptation (reduced water stress), but that is not the single biggest climate-relevant effect universally; the consistent cross-context climate linkage is reduced energy/emissions from lower water volumes.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that implements smart metering + consumption-based billing/feedback aims to reduce per-capita water use through information and price signals, producing the same efficiency-led pathway even if local energy intensity of water varies.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the resulting benefit is mainly utility-side electricity savings, customer hot-water energy savings, or adaptation benefits depends on local system characteristics and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the jurisdiction’s water supply/wastewater energy intensity is very low (e.g., gravity-fed, low-treatment) and hot-water impacts are minimal, climate mitigation benefits may be small and the policy may function more as water-security adaptation; however, the mechanism as defined is still primarily an efficiency-driven demand-reduction pathway.
key_indicators: "automated"; "hour-based"; "remotely readable"; "measurement of water consumption"; "recommends consumption-based invoicing"
co_benefits: Reduced non-revenue water/leak losses; improved billing accuracy and fairness; operational efficiencies for the utility; customer awareness/behavior change; potential affordability impacts (positive or negative) depending on tariff design.
instance_edge_case_notes: “HSY recommends consumption-based invoicing” points toward a pricing/billing reform (pricing-mechanism), but because it is phrased as a recommendation and the operative commitment is metering automation/remote readability, the instrument is classified as infrastructure-investment/enabling rather than a pricing mechanism.
parent_policy_name: Key actions related to new energy solutions.

policy_statement: Investigate, pilot and adopt two-way district heating in City properties.
parent_statement: Key actions related to new energy solutions.
verbatim_text: 76. Two-way district heating in the City properties will be investigated, piloted and adopted.
extraction_rationale: Clear technology deployment pathway (investigate → pilot → adopt).
mechanism_description: The City will investigate, pilot and adopt two-way district heating in its properties to reduce fossil fuel use for heating and lower associated greenhouse gas emissions.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]; row context missing primary/climate token: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Building heat provision shift → lower-carbon/more efficient heat supply → ↓ fossil fuel combustion for heating → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Buildings connect to district heating/thermal energy networks (including potential two-way networks) instead of relying on individual on-site boilers or electric resistance heating, and heat production is centralized/shared across multiple buildings.  
(2) Mediating physical process: The network enables higher conversion efficiency and operational optimization at scale and allows incorporation of low-carbon heat sources (industrial/wastewater waste heat, geothermal, ambient loops with heat pumps, CHP transitioning to renewables, large thermal storage), reducing combustion of natural gas/oil and associated methane/CO₂ emissions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced direct and indirect GHG emissions from space and water heating in connected buildings (and potentially enabling future decarbonization as the network’s heat sources get cleaner over time).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is lowering GHG emissions from building space and water heating by shifting heat supply to a more efficient and lower-carbon shared network.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s core purpose is to decarbonize building heating by changing heat supply infrastructure, thereby reducing fossil combustion and GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While district heating can improve energy efficiency (a resource-efficiency co-benefit) and can marginally aid resilience via diversified supply, the largest consistent climate effect across policies using this mechanism is emissions mitigation from cleaner heat.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a district heating network is supplied mainly by unabated fossil CHP/boilers, near-term mitigation benefits may be smaller; however, the mechanism as defined (“more efficient and lower-carbon heat sources”) is still principally aimed at emissions reduction, not adaptation or nature-based outcomes.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that deploys district heating/thermal energy networks to serve buildings shares the same dominant mitigation pathway—shifting heating provision to a shared system that can reduce fossil heat emissions.

4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that recovers and utilizes landfill gas necessarily targets methane capture/control at the landfill, yielding mitigation benefits regardless of whether energy utilization is implemented.  
5) Note on subtypes: Secondary categorization (e.g., “renewable energy generation,” “waste methane management,” “resource recovery”) is deferred to policy-specific evidence such as whether gas is flared vs. used for electricity/heat or upgraded to RNG.

policy_statement: Increase environmental education in early childhood education and schools.
parent_statement: Key actions related to education.
verbatim_text: 92. The amount of environmental education in early childhood education and schools will be increased.
extraction_rationale: Clear educational intervention commitment.
mechanism_description: It will increase environmental education in early childhood education and schools to build climate awareness that supports pro-climate behaviors and decisions over time.
primary_causal_pathway: Education/awareness intervention → behavior & decision change → ↓ fossil energy/material demand + ↑ support for mitigation policy → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the human system by integrating environmental/climate education into early childhood and school curricula, teacher practice, and school culture, shaping students’ knowledge, values, and norms. (2) This mediates impact through social/behavioral processes (attitude formation, norm diffusion, skill-building, political and consumer preference changes) that influence household consumption, mobility, energy use, and later professional/institutional decisions. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is lower emissions (and potentially faster adoption of mitigation measures) due to sustained pro-climate behaviors and increased societal willingness to implement decarbonization policies.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions over time by increasing climate literacy that drives lower-carbon choices and stronger public support for mitigation actions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: education → behavioral/political support shifts → lower-carbon choices and enabling of emissions-reducing policies → ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while education can incidentally promote “resource efficiency” behaviors (e.g., waste reduction), the overarching and largest climate effect of this mechanism is emissions mitigation via demand-side change and policy support, not direct efficiency technology change or climate hazard risk reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: if an education program were explicitly framed/implemented mainly as disaster preparedness or heat-risk awareness, it could lean Adaptation; however, this canonical mechanism is explicitly “pro-climate behavior and support,” which is mitigation-dominant.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: any policy that increases environmental/climate education to build awareness with the aim of pro-climate behaviors/support shares the same primary mitigation pathway (indirect but emissions-relevant).  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., demand-side behavior change vs. governance/enabling) is deferred to policy-level evidence and quantified intent/implementation details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Indirect-impact mechanism with long time lags and attribution uncertainty; some implementations may primarily target adaptation awareness (e.g., local hazard preparedness) or general environmental outcomes not tightly linked to GHG reductions—those would require policy-specific review.
key_indicators: “environmental education”; “early childhood education and schools”; “will be increased”
co_benefits: Improved general environmental literacy; potential public health and local environmental quality benefits via reduced pollution-related behaviors.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a regulatory-mandate if the underlying action is a compulsory curriculum requirement; the text only states an increase in education without specifying mandate/standard-setting, so it is treated as an information/education instrument.
primary_gate_reason: no Adaptation outcome token found in source; flagging for review
parent_policy_name: Key actions related to education.

The primary gate failure ('no Mitigation outcome token found') is consistent: the text contains no direct emissions-reduction/energy/transport/industry mitigation outcome. It is still climate-relevant via the stated education mechanism ("environmental education" -> pro-climate behavior/support). Therefore keep in dataset but reclassify away from direct Mitigation.

policy_statement: Organise courses and education on climate change mitigation and adaptation, circular economy and sharing economy for City employees, representatives and residents.
parent_statement: Key actions related to education.

extraction_rationale: Specific training programme commitment with defined topics and audiences.

primary_causal_pathway: Capacity-building/knowledge → improved preparedness & decision-making → ↓ vulnerability/↑ adaptive capacity to climate hazards
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed human activity/system: The city provides training, courses, and education for staff and residents on climate mitigation/adaptation and related practices (e.g., circular/sharing economy), increasing awareness, competencies, and organizational readiness.  
2) Mediating process: Improved knowledge, skills, and governance capacity leads to better risk recognition, planning, coordination, and uptake of protective practices (e.g., emergency preparedness, heat-risk protocols, resilient operations).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced vulnerability and improved ability to cope with and recover from climate impacts (higher adaptive capacity), with potential co-benefits of increased mitigation action uptake.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is strengthening adaptive capacity so communities and institutions can better anticipate, plan for, and respond to climate hazards.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Education/training primarily functions as an enabling measure that increases preparedness and the capability to implement climate responses, i.e., adaptive capacity.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although training can motivate emissions-reducing behaviors (mitigation) and promote circular economy practices (resource efficiency), those outcomes are indirect and variable; the most consistent, mechanism-level climate effect is improved capacity to adapt and respond to hazards.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program is explicitly designed and evaluated mainly to deliver quantified emissions reductions (e.g., targeted retrofitting workforce training tied to building efficiency outcomes), that would shift the dominant pathway toward mitigation/resource efficiency—but that is a different, narrower mechanism than general climate education/capacity-building.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any “climate education and training → action uptake and adaptive capacity” program inherently targets knowledge/capacity as the lever, which most broadly maps to adaptation via increased adaptive capacity.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy instance is secondarily mitigation- or resource-efficiency-relevant depends on the specific curriculum, target audiences, and monitored outcomes, which must be assessed at the policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Education/training is an enabling instrument that can support multiple categories; classification hinges on the dominant, most general effect. Programs narrowly aimed at emissions outcomes (e.g., training installers for heat pumps) may warrant mitigation/resource-efficiency classification under a different canonical mechanism.
key_indicators: "will organise courses and education"; "mitigation of and adaptation to climate change"; "for its employees, representatives and residents"
co_benefits: Improved mitigation action uptake (via education on "mitigation", "circular economy" and "sharing economy")
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy (capacity-building program), but the operative mechanism is education/knowledge provision, best captured as information-disclosure.

policy_statement: Promote urban agriculture by crowdsourcing a survey of suitable spaces and defining criteria and rules for their use.
parent_statement: Key actions related to education.
verbatim_text: 94. Urban agriculture will be promoted by using crowdsourcing to survey spaces suitable to be used by the residents. The criteria for spaces suitable for urban agriculture and the rules for the use of these spaces will be defined.
extraction_rationale: Concrete intervention: space survey and rule/criteria setting to enable urban agriculture.
mechanism_description: It promotes urban agriculture by crowdsourcing a survey to identify suitable spaces and by defining criteria and rules for using those spaces, enabling more urban food growing and related resilience co-benefits.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard/shock (heat, storms, supply-chain disruption) → ↑ local food production capacity + redundancy → ↓ vulnerability / ↑ adaptive capacity (food security resilience)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city enables and governs use of suitable urban spaces for food growing (identifying sites, setting access/eligibility criteria, and rules for cultivation), increasing participation in urban agriculture.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological/social process: Expanded distributed food production and community networks increase redundancy in food supply; vegetated plots can modestly moderate microclimate and improve water infiltration depending on design.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced sensitivity to climate-driven supply disruptions and extreme events (improved food security and community adaptive capacity), with possible secondary mitigation co-benefits that are not the dominant, guaranteed outcome.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improving resilience to climate-related disruptions by increasing local food production capacity and related community preparedness.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: enabling local, distributed food production increases adaptive capacity to climate shocks (especially supply-chain disruption and extreme weather), which is an adaptation outcome.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: mitigation from “food miles” reduction or soil carbon gains is variable and often modest; the consistent effect across policies using this mechanism is resilience/food security benefits.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a program explicitly targets net GHG reductions (e.g., quantified reductions, high-impact compost-to-soil carbon projects, replacing emissions-intensive imports at scale), mitigation could become dominant—but that is not inherent to this mechanism as stated.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: policies that survey/identify spaces and set rules/criteria to enable urban agriculture generally aim to increase local growing capacity and resilience benefits, making Adaptation the best fit at the mechanism level.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: any mitigation (reduced transport, waste diversion via composting, soil carbon sequestration) or nature-based cooling benefits require policy-specific evidence and are deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Urban agriculture can sometimes be framed primarily as Nature-Based Solutions (heat mitigation/green cover) or Mitigation (soil carbon/composting, reduced supply-chain emissions) if explicitly designed and measured for those outcomes; absent such specificity, resilience/adaptive capacity is the most general dominant pathway for this mechanism.
key_indicators: “promoted by using crowdsourcing to survey spaces”; “criteria for spaces suitable for urban agriculture”; “rules for the use of these spaces will be defined”
co_benefits: Potential mitigation via reduced food miles; urban heat microclimate benefits from added vegetation; community cohesion and public health benefits.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a regulatory instrument because it mentions “rules,” but the core action is preparatory planning (inventorying spaces and defining eligibility/use criteria) rather than a binding performance requirement or mandate.
parent_policy_name: Key actions related to education.

policy_statement: Key actions related to consumption and waste management.
verbatim_text: Key actions related to consumption and waste management
extraction_rationale: Introduces numbered actions (95–100) under consumption and waste.
mechanism_description: The policy references actions on consumption and waste management, but it does not describe which measures will be used or how they would reduce emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste/consumption system actions → ↓ waste-related CH₄/CO₂ (direct + upstream) → ↓ net GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Municipal/community consumption patterns and waste management practices (generation, collection, treatment, disposal), though the specific interventions are not stated.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced biodegradable waste landfilled (lower anaerobic decomposition and methane formation), increased diversion/treatment efficiency, and/or reduced material throughput leading to lower lifecycle emissions from production and disposal.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower net greenhouse gas emissions (especially methane from landfills and CO₂ across the waste/material lifecycle).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing net GHG emissions from the waste/consumption system (most commonly by avoiding landfill methane and/or upstream production emissions).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism asserts “waste management/consumption actions” leading to “emissions reduction,” which is a direct mitigation intent (↓ GHG emissions).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some waste actions can improve resource efficiency (same service with less material/energy) or have adaptation co-benefits, the stated outcome is emissions reduction, making Mitigation the dominant and unifying category.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the unspecified actions were primarily about resilience (e.g., flood-proofing waste facilities) the category could shift to Adaptation, but that is not indicated; if framed as “resource efficiency” without emissions language it could shift categories, but here emissions reduction is explicit.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: By definition, this canonical mechanism groups policies that mention waste/consumption actions with unspecified measures but explicitly link them to emissions reduction; that linkage consistently implies Mitigation as the primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: More specific sub-mechanisms (e.g., landfill methane avoidance vs. recycling lifecycle benefits vs. waste-to-energy) require policy-level evidence and are intentionally deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Because measures are unspecified, the actual dominant emissions pathway could vary (landfill methane avoidance vs. lifecycle material reduction vs. energy recovery). If future text reveals the policy is primarily circular-economy efficiency (without explicit GHG framing) or primarily resilience of waste services, reclassification may be warranted; with current wording, Mitigation remains the best fit.
key_indicators: “Key actions”; “consumption”; “waste management”
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollution/odors, improved public health and sanitation, resource efficiency/circular economy benefits, potential cost savings
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument is underspecified: could map to targets, infrastructure investment, pricing (PAYT), or regulatory diversion requirements if the underlying “key actions” were enumerated; classified as planning-strategy by default given only a headline statement.

policy_statement: Develop recipes to reduce emissions from City food services and protect the Baltic Sea.
parent_statement: Key actions related to consumption and waste management.
verbatim_text: 95. Recipes will be developed to reduce the emissions from the City's food services and protect the Baltic Sea.
extraction_rationale: Specific intervention in public food services to reduce emissions.
mechanism_description: Recipes will be developed for the City’s food services to shift meals toward lower-emission options, reducing food-related greenhouse gas emissions while also supporting Baltic Sea protection.
primary_causal_pathway: Food procurement/menu choices → ↓ demand for high-emission foods → ↓ agricultural + supply-chain GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: City-operated food services (schools, hospitals, workplaces, etc.) redesign recipes and menus to substitute lower-emission ingredients/meals for higher-emission ones.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced demand for emissions-intensive agricultural production (especially enteric methane from ruminants, manure-related CH₄/N₂O, fertilizer-related N₂O) and reduced processing/transport emissions across the food supply chain.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower total consumption-based GHG emissions associated with municipal food service operations (primarily CH₄ and N₂O from agriculture, plus CO₂ from energy/transport).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced life-cycle greenhouse gas emissions from food production and supply chains due to shifting menus toward lower-emission foods.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Menu/procurement shifts reduce upstream food-system GHGs; the main effect is emissions reduction rather than efficiency, hazard reduction, or ecosystem restoration.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although “resource efficiency” can be relevant (e.g., reducing food waste) and “nature-based” co-benefits can occur, the core mechanism here is dietary/procurement substitution to cut life-cycle emissions—i.e., mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If implemented mainly as nutrient runoff reduction for Baltic Sea protection, the primary objective could appear environmental/water-quality; however the stated mechanism explicitly targets emissions reductions, and even when framed as co-benefits, the dominant climate effect remains GHG mitigation via demand shift.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that develops low-carbon recipes/menus for food services operates through the same demand/substitution channel to reduce food-system GHG emissions, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any finer classification (e.g., “methane reduction,” “consumption-based accounting,” “public procurement”) should be assigned at the individual policy level based on the specific implementation details and evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a particular program’s implemented changes are primarily to reduce eutrophication (e.g., phosphorus/nitrogen) with minimal actual dietary GHG shift, climate benefits could be secondary; otherwise straightforward mitigation via food-demand substitution.
key_indicators: “Recipes will be developed”; “reduce the emissions”; “City's food services”; “protect the Baltic Sea”
co_benefits: Reduced nutrient runoff/eutrophication impacts supporting Baltic Sea protection; potential public health benefits from dietary shifts
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a procurement/menu standard if the “recipes” are later mandated/embedded in catering specifications, but the text only states development (guidance/planning) rather than a requirement.
parent_policy_name: Key actions related to consumption and waste management.

policy_statement: Increase the proportion of vegetarian meals in schools and day care centres.
parent_statement: Key actions related to consumption and waste management.
verbatim_text: 96. The proportion of vegetarian meals will be increased in schools and day care centres.
extraction_rationale: Concrete demand-side food policy change in public institutions.
mechanism_description: Increasing the share of vegetarian meals in schools and day care centres shifts consumption away from emissions-intensive animal products, reducing food-related greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Dietary demand shift → ↓ livestock production & associated inputs → ↓ CH₄/N₂O + ↓ CO₂ (land-use/energy) → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: Public food procurement and consumption patterns in schools/day care shift toward vegetarian meals, reducing per-meal animal-product content.  
(2) Mediating process: Lower demand for livestock products reduces enteric fermentation methane, manure-related methane and nitrous oxide, feed production emissions (fertilizer N₂O, fuel use), and—depending on supply response—land conversion/land-use emissions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in food-related lifecycle GHG emissions attributable to the served meals (and potentially broader market/behavior spillovers).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced food-system GHG emissions by lowering demand for emissions-intensive animal products (especially ruminant meat and dairy).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Changing diets/procurement reduces upstream agricultural and land-use-related GHG emissions; this is a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be resource-efficiency co-benefits (lower land/water intensity) and potential nature-based effects (reduced land pressure), the principal intended and largest climate effect across policies using this mechanism is reduced GHG emissions from food production.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the plant-based shift is implemented via food-waste reduction or composting, that would be a different mechanism; here the lever is substitution away from animal products. If plant-based options are highly processed and energy-intensive, mitigation benefits may be smaller but the dominant pathway remains emissions reduction relative to typical animal-based meals.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that increases plant-based/vegetarian meal share to displace animal products relies on the same core emissions-reduction pathway in the food system.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific mitigation subtypes (e.g., agricultural CH₄ reduction vs. land-use CO₂ vs. procurement/behavior) depend on policy design and evidence and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Benefits depend on what foods are displaced (largest for beef/lamb/dairy) and on supply-chain response; if substitution is mainly from low-emission animal products (e.g., poultry) to high-footprint plant products or if rebound increases consumption elsewhere, net reductions could be reduced, but the dominant intended mechanism remains mitigation via lower food-system GHGs.
key_indicators: “proportion of vegetarian meals will be increased”; “in schools and day care centres”
co_benefits: Potential public health/nutrition benefits; reduced local air pollution and water/land-use pressures from livestock supply chains.
instance_edge_case_notes: No explicit mandate wording (e.g., “shall/required”) or numeric target is provided, but the stated increase in the proportion of vegetarian meals in public settings functions as an institutional menu/procurement requirement.

policy_statement: Reduce food waste in Service Centre, Palmia and other stakeholder operations through monitoring consumption, campaigns, process efficiency and rationalising product selection/pack sizes.
parent_statement: Key actions related to consumption and waste management.
verbatim_text:
97. Food waste will be reduced in the operations of the Service Centre, Palmia and other interest groups.

· For example: by monitoring consumption ... by using campaigns, by making the processes more efficient and by rationalising the product selection and the package sizes.
extraction_rationale: Specific operational measures to cut food waste.
mechanism_description: The city will reduce food waste in stakeholder operations through monitoring, campaigns, process improvements, and product/pack-size changes, lowering organic waste disposal and avoiding landfill methane emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste generation/diversion changes → ↓ organic waste to landfill → ↓ anaerobic decomposition → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ radiative forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Households, businesses, and city services prevent and/or divert food and other organics away from landfill (e.g., source reduction, redistribution, separate collection, composting/anaerobic digestion, circular business practices that reduce discards).  
(2) Mediating process: With less biodegradable carbon entering landfill conditions, there is less anaerobic microbial breakdown that produces methane over time (and potentially less landfill gas generation overall).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower direct GHG emissions, dominated by reduced CH₄ (and some CO₂) from waste disposal, yielding net mitigation benefits.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided landfill methane emissions from reduced organic waste disposal.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s main physical effect is reducing methane formation by keeping organics out of landfills, directly lowering GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: While “circular economy” and “zero-waste routes” can also improve material efficiency, the mechanism specified (and common across the examples) is explicitly oriented toward avoiding landfill CH₄—an emissions reduction outcome rather than primarily reducing resource/energy inputs for the same service.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some circular-economy policies may yield larger upstream manufacturing/energy savings than landfill CH₄ reductions depending on materials targeted; however, this canonical mechanism is defined as landfill methane avoidance via waste diversion/food waste reduction, so Mitigation remains primary for all policies that truly share this mechanism.  
4) Confirm applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism necessarily reduces organic landfill disposal and therefore reduces landfill methane potential; that is inherently a mitigation pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a given policy’s secondary benefits are primarily “Resource Efficiency” (e.g., avoided production emissions) or include other co-benefits is policy- and implementation-dependent and should be assigned only with policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some “circular economy/zero-waste” initiatives may be dominated by upstream embodied-emissions reductions (resource efficiency) if they focus on non-organic materials; ensure the policy actually reduces organic landfill inputs when mapping to this mechanism. If a policy instead centers on landfill gas capture rather than diversion, it belongs to a different mechanism.
key_indicators: "Food waste will be reduced"; "monitoring consumption"; "using campaigns"; "making the processes more efficient"; "rationalising the product selection and the package sizes"
co_benefits: Cost savings from reduced purchasing and disposal; operational efficiency improvements; potential social benefits if edible surplus is redirected (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be coded as an information/behavior-change effort (“campaigns”) but the overall package is broader operational waste-prevention planning rather than pure disclosure. No explicit quantitative target or binding requirement is stated.

policy_statement: Examine opportunities for low-emission food transport (e.g., climate-friendly long-life packaging; more efficient deliveries).

98. The opportunities to transport food optimally in terms of emissions will be examined.

• For example: climate-friendly and long-life packag- ing; making deliveries more efficient.
extraction_rationale: Explicit commitment to examine/logistics improvements to reduce emissions.
mechanism_description: The policy examines climate-friendly packaging and more efficient food deliveries to lower emissions from food transport.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Freight delivery operations → ↓ vehicle-km and/or ↓ fuel per delivery → ↓ combustion emissions (CO₂e) → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: How goods (especially food) are packaged, routed, consolidated, and scheduled for delivery to reduce trips, increase load factors, and improve routing efficiency (and sometimes enable mode/vehicle shifts).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced internal combustion energy use (diesel/gasoline) and/or reduced electricity demand for freight movement per unit delivered; fewer cold-chain losses may also reduce upstream production emissions if waste is avoided.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower direct transport CO₂ emissions (and potentially lower NOx/black carbon co-emissions), yielding net GHG reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced freight/delivery fuel consumption leading to lower transportation GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Freight/delivery efficiency reduces fuel burned (or energy used) per delivered unit and/or reduces total delivery travel, directly cutting transport-sector GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “efficiency” can resemble Resource Efficiency, the dominant climate outcome here is explicitly emissions reduction from transportation energy use (Mitigation), not primarily conserving materials/water.  
3) Edge cases applied: If “climate-friendly packaging” mainly meant lower-embodied-carbon materials, that would lean toward Resource Efficiency/industrial mitigation; however, the described chain centers on enabling more efficient deliveries (transport emissions), so Mitigation remains dominant for this mechanism.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism (freight/delivery efficiency → transport emissions reduction) has its main climate effect through reduced transport energy use and emissions, so Mitigation applies broadly.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy is chiefly routing optimization, consolidation hubs, procurement, packaging-driven logistics changes, or mode/vehicle shift is determined at the individual policy level and is not set here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Packaging-focused actions could shift the dominant impact toward embodied emissions/material efficiency if logistics effects are minor; food-waste prevention effects could also become material if shelf-life packaging significantly reduces upstream agricultural emissions.
key_indicators: “opportunities to transport food optimally in terms of emissions will be examined”; “climate-friendly and long-life packaging”; “making deliveries more efficient”
co_benefits: Reduced food waste/spoilage (via long-life packaging); potential air quality improvements from reduced freight activity (NOx/PM); cost savings from logistics efficiency
instance_edge_case_notes: No concrete instrument (e.g., mandate, incentive, investment) is specified—only an intention to “examine” options—so classification leans to planning/strategy rather than performance standard or incentive program.

policy_statement: Increase collaboration with grocery shops to reduce food waste, including transferring excess food to community initiatives and institutions (e.g., Shared Table).

99. Collaboration with grocery shops will be increased to reduce food waste.

- For example: by transferring excess food from grocery shops to ... or the Shared Table initiative.
extraction_rationale: Concrete collaboration action to divert surplus food and reduce waste.
mechanism_description: It increases collaboration with grocery shops to transfer excess food to community initiatives and institutions, reducing food waste and associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Food system demand reduction → ↓ food production + ↓ supply-chain energy use + ↓ organic waste to landfill → ↓ CO₂e (incl. ↓ CH₄) → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Households, retailers, institutions, and food service reduce edible food discards through prevention, redistribution, and improved inventory/portioning/planning.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological processes: Less food demanded and discarded reduces upstream agricultural emissions (fertilizer-related N₂O, enteric/land-use CO₂, on-farm energy), processing/cold-chain/transport energy (CO₂), and reduces anaerobic decomposition of organics in landfills (CH₄).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions (CO₂e), often dominated by avoided upstream production emissions, with additional landfill methane avoidance when diversion/prevention reduces landfilled organics.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding GHG emissions by preventing the production and supply-chain activities of food that would otherwise be wasted (with additional avoided landfill methane where applicable).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Reduced food waste lowers total food system throughput, yielding avoided lifecycle GHG emissions (upstream) and often reduced landfill CH₄—this is a direct emissions-reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it also improves resource efficiency (less input per delivered nutrition) and can reduce waste management burdens, the mechanism’s central climate-relevant outcome is lower CO₂e emissions, which maps best to Mitigation rather than Resource Efficiency as the primary category.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy focuses only on diverting food waste to composting/AD without preventing waste, the dominant mechanism may shift toward “organic waste diversion → reduced landfill methane” (still Mitigation). If framed mainly as food security/social support, climate benefits may be secondary, but the shared mechanism as defined is emissions avoidance.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is “food waste reduction → upstream emissions + landfill methane reduction” inherently targets lower GHG emissions from avoided production and/or avoided landfill CH₄, making Mitigation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the dominant emissions savings come more from upstream agricultural avoidance vs. landfill methane avoidance (and whether redistribution vs. prevention is emphasized) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some “food waste” policies emphasize diversion (composting/anaerobic digestion) rather than prevention; those remain Mitigation but would be better represented by a different canonical mechanism. Additionally, if reduced waste leads to rebound consumption elsewhere, net upstream savings could be smaller; this does not change the primary category for the mechanism.

co_benefits: Improved food security/poverty alleviation via food redistribution; reduced municipal solid waste management burdens/costs
instance_edge_case_notes: Framed as “collaboration” without specifying whether incentives/funding are provided; classified as an incentive-program/enabling partnership because it centers on facilitating voluntary redistribution rather than imposing requirements.

extraction_rationale: Specific standards update plus tool development and system adoption encouragement.
mechanism_description: The city updates environmental criteria for events, encourages use of an environmental management system, and develops tools to measure event CO2 emissions to support lower-emission event planning and delivery.
primary_causal_pathway: Event governance/management set + emissions measurement → operational changes in event logistics/energy/transport/materials → ↓ fossil energy use + ↓ waste-related emissions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: How events are planned, procured, and operated (criteria/standards for organizers and suppliers; adoption of an environmental management system; carbon accounting for events).  
2) Mediating process: Measurement and management create accountability and identify hotspots, prompting substitutions and efficiency measures (e.g., renewable/efficient venue energy, low-carbon transport options, reduced material throughput, higher reuse/recycling, reduced food-related emissions).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower direct and indirect event-related GHG emissions (Scopes vary by tool/policy), contributing to mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from events (especially attendee/travel, venue energy use, and materials/waste) due to better environmental management and emissions measurement.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Governance measures (criteria + EMS + emissions measurement) lead to concrete operational changes that reduce event-related GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the mechanism is “enabling” and could improve resource efficiency, the dominant intended climate effect across policies using this mechanism is emissions reduction (mitigation) rather than adaptation or nature-based outcomes.  
3) Edge cases applied: If implemented only as reporting without any linkage to decisions, emissions reductions could be weak; however the mechanism explicitly couples criteria/EMS with measurement to enable reductions, keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that updates event environmental criteria and measures event emissions to support lower-emission planning targets the same primary outcome—reduced event GHG emissions—so Mitigation remains the primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether reductions come mainly from energy, transport, waste, catering, or procurement depends on the specific policy design and evidence at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be classified as Resource Efficiency if the mechanism were strictly focused on reducing material/energy use without an emissions-reduction objective; could also be “enabling” with negligible mitigation if purely voluntary reporting is not tied to operational change. In the stated mechanism, emissions measurement and criteria are explicitly aimed at lower-emission events, so Mitigation is appropriate.
key_indicators: "environmental criteria for events will be updated"; "materials to help with the planning"; "encouraged to use the EcoCompass environmental management system"; "Tools for measuring the CO2 emissions of events will be developed"
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollution and noise from event logistics/transport; improved waste management and litter reduction; potential cost savings from resource efficiency
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be classified partly as information-disclosure if CO2 measurement is paired with public reporting, and partly as regulatory-mandate/performance-standard if the updated criteria become binding permit conditions; the text emphasizes encouragement/tool development rather than enforceable requirements.
parent_policy_name: Key actions related to consumption and waste management.

policy_statement: Helsinki aims to become carbon-neutral by 2035 by reducing greenhouse gas emissions 80% from 1990 levels and compensating the remaining 20% through emissions reductions outside the city.
verbatim_text: The objective of the Helsinki City Strategy 2017-2021 is to make Helsinki  carbon-neutral  by  2035. This  goal will be achieved by reducing the greenhouse gas emissions in Helsinki by 80 per cent. The remaining  20  per  cent  will  be  compensated for by Helsinki taking care of implementing  emissions  reductions  outside  the  city.
extraction_rationale: Explicit quantified target (carbon-neutral by 2035; -80% reduction; 20% compensation) stated as a city strategy objective.
mechanism_description: The city sets a carbon-neutrality goal and plans to cut local greenhouse gas emissions while compensating remaining emissions through emissions reductions outside the city.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Policy target-setting + governance → cross-sector decarbonization actions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city adopts a binding or guiding community-wide emissions target (e.g., % reduction by a year, net-zero), aligning planning, budgeting, regulations, and investments across energy, buildings, transport, waste, and industry.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels, improved energy efficiency, fuel switching/electrification, methane reductions, and other sector measures lower the mass flow of GHGs released to the atmosphere.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower community-wide GHG emissions (CO₂e) leading to reduced cumulative atmospheric GHG concentrations and reduced radiative forcing over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is achieving economy-wide reductions in greenhouse gas emissions (often to net-zero) across major sectors.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s central function is to set an economy-wide emissions outcome that triggers and coordinates decarbonization measures, yielding ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While targets can indirectly improve efficiency or resilience, their defining and largest intended climate effect is emissions mitigation at the community scale, not adaptation (hazard/exposure reduction), not resource efficiency as an end in itself, and not ecosystem-based carbon sequestration as the primary lever.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some targets include “net-zero” via offsets or land-sector sinks; even then, the dominant climate-relevant purpose remains achieving net emissions reductions/neutrality (mitigation). If a document is purely aspirational with no implementation linkage, it is still categorized by intended dominant pathway (mitigation).  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that sets a community-wide GHG reduction or net-zero target shares the same primary mechanism—economy-wide emissions reduction—regardless of sector mix or local context.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether delivery relies more on efficiency, electrification, renewables, waste methane controls, offsets, or nature-based sinks requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Targets framed mainly as “climate neutrality” can be met with offsets or external credits; classification remains Mitigation, but downstream interpretation should note whether reductions are in-boundary vs. compensated. Targets limited to municipal operations (not community-wide) would be a different mechanism and should not be conflated.
key_indicators: "carbon-neutral by 2035"; "reducing the greenhouse gas emissions… by 80 per cent"; "remaining 20 per cent will be compensated"; "emissions reductions outside the city"; "objective… City Strategy 2017-2021"
co_benefits: None
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes an offsetting/compensation component (“emissions reductions outside the city”), but the instrument remains target-setting (no specific regulatory, pricing, or investment instrument is specified in the excerpt).

policy_statement: Helsinki sets a traffic-sector goal to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from traffic by 69% from 2005 levels by 2035.
verbatim_text: To  reach  the  goal  of  becoming carbon-neutral, the traffic emissions were set a separate goal: the objective is  to  reduce  greenhouse  gas  emissions  by 69 per cent from the level of 2005 by 2035
extraction_rationale: Quantified sectoral target with baseline year and deadline.
mechanism_description: The city sets a goal to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from traffic, creating a pathway for measures that lower transport-sector emissions over time.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Transport system governance/targets → implementation of emissions-reducing measures → ↓ fossil fuel combustion (and other transport GHGs) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city adopts quantified transport GHG reduction targets (often with interim milestones) that steer planning, budgeting, regulation, and project selection in the transport sector.  
2) Mediating process: The target catalyzes and legitimizes interventions (e.g., electrification, mode shift, operational efficiency, pricing, land-use/transport integration) that reduce vehicle kilometers traveled, improve energy efficiency per km, and/or lower carbon intensity of energy used.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in transport-related CO₂e emissions within the city boundary or inventory scope (and potentially reduced upstream emissions depending on accounting).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is reducing transport-sector GHG emissions (CO₂e) by driving and coordinating measures that lower fuel combustion and/or travel demand over time.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: A sectoral transport GHG target primarily functions to achieve ↓ GHG emissions by driving a suite of transport decarbonization actions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some resulting measures can also improve energy productivity (resource efficiency) or reduce heat/pollution exposure (adaptation/co-benefits), the stated objective and main climate-relevant consequence across examples is emissions reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “target” were framed around resilience (e.g., maintaining mobility during climate shocks) it would be adaptation, but that is a different mechanism; here the target is explicitly GHG/emissions-focused.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy that sets a transport-sector GHG reduction goal/target shares the same dominant intent and effect—reducing transport emissions—regardless of which specific measures are later used to meet it.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the target is achieved via electrification, mode shift, VMT reduction, freight measures, or fuel switching is policy- and implementation-specific and should be assigned at the individual policy level with supporting evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the target is purely aspirational with no linkage to implementation, the realized mitigation impact may be weak, but the mechanism’s intended dominant pathway remains mitigation via transport emissions reduction; accounting scope (territorial vs lifecycle) can also affect measured outcomes but not the primary category.
key_indicators: “traffic emissions were set a separate goal”; “objective is to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 69 per cent”; “from the level of 2005 by 2035”; “becoming carbon-neutral”
co_benefits: Potential air quality improvement, reduced noise, congestion reduction, public health benefits from mode shift (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Target is presented as a goal/objective without explicit legal binding force or accompanying compliance/MRV provisions in the excerpt; classified as target-setting rather than regulatory mandate or planning strategy.
